cyber.title: Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS) keeps attacks cheap and frequent
cyber.summary: Criminal crews now rent ready-made ransomware to affiliates, so even low-skill actors can launch attacks. Defense leans on offline backups, network segmentation, MFA, and fast detection rather than hoping to prevent every intrusion.

cyber.title: Phishing & business email compromise remain the #1 way in
cyber.summary: Most breaches still start with a convincing email that steals a credential or redirects a payment. Phishing-resistant MFA (passkeys), DMARC, and payment-verification steps cut the damage far more than awareness training alone.

cyber.title: Software supply-chain attacks hit many victims at once
cyber.summary: Compromising one dependency or build pipeline (npm/PyPI typosquats, SolarWinds-style) reaches everyone downstream. Mitigations: pin and verify dependencies, sign artifacts, and ship an SBOM so you know what's in your software.

cyber.title: Zero-days are weaponized within days — patch velocity matters
cyber.summary: Attackers exploit newly disclosed flaws fast, so 'patch later' is risky. CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog tells you which bugs are actively used in the wild and should jump the patch queue.

cyber.title: Infostealers harvest saved passwords and session tokens
cyber.summary: Malware that quietly grabs browser-stored credentials and cookies fuels account takeover at scale. Passkeys, short-lived tokens, and device trust shrink what a stolen secret can unlock.

cyber.title: Cloud misconfiguration is still a top breach cause
cyber.summary: Public storage buckets, over-broad IAM roles, and secrets left in code expose data without any 'hack'. Least-privilege access, automated config scanning, and secret detection in CI catch most of it.

cyber.title: Zero Trust replaces the old network perimeter
cyber.summary: Instead of trusting anything inside the firewall, every request is authenticated and authorized on identity and device posture. NIST SP 800-207 is the reference architecture organizations are adopting.

cyber.title: AI-powered phishing and deepfakes raise the bar
cyber.summary: LLMs make phishing flawless and enable convincing voice/video impersonation of executives. Verification call-backs, signed communications, and out-of-band approval for money movement are the practical defenses.

cyber.title: Prompt injection is the new injection class for AI apps
cyber.summary: Untrusted text can hijack an LLM's instructions and make agents misuse their tools. The OWASP LLM Top 10 lists it as the #1 risk; treat all model input as untrusted and constrain tool access.

ai.title: RAG grounds AI answers in your own data
ai.summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation fetches your current documents and feeds them to the model, so answers stay accurate, up-to-date, and sourced instead of hallucinated. It's the default pattern for enterprise AI.

ai.title: AI agents move from chatting to doing
ai.summary: An agent is an LLM given tools that runs a reason-act-observe loop: it plans a step, calls an API or runs code, sees the result, and repeats until the task is done. The leap is autonomy under human direction.

ai.title: Multi-agent systems split work across specialists
ai.summary: Complex tasks are handled by an orchestrator delegating to focused sub-agents (research, code, review), sidestepping single-context limits. More capable, but more coordination to get right.

ai.title: MCP is becoming the standard plug for AI tools
ai.summary: The Model Context Protocol is an open standard that connects AI apps to tools and data uniformly — 'USB-C for AI'. Instead of custom integrations, models speak MCP to any compliant server.

ai.title: Small language models complement the big ones
ai.summary: Smaller, task-specific models are cheaper, faster, and can run privately. The emerging pattern mixes one big general model with many small specialized ones for better economics and data control.

ai.title: Evals are unit tests for AI
ai.summary: Because model output is non-deterministic, teams gate quality with systematic evaluations of accuracy, safety, and format. Evals plus guardrails are the line between a flashy demo and a production system.

ai.title: Agentic coding reshapes how software is built
ai.summary: AI now plans features, edits across files, runs tests, and opens pull requests. Developers shift from writing every line to directing and reviewing agents — handling broader scope, faster.

ai.title: Vector databases power semantic search & RAG
ai.summary: Embeddings turn text into meaning-vectors; vector databases (pgvector, Pinecone, and others) find the most relevant items by meaning rather than keywords — the storage layer behind RAG.

ai.title: AI governance & safety move from optional to expected
ai.summary: As agents take real actions, organizations add guardrails, human-in-the-loop approval, and audit trails. Governing model access and data (including MCP-server sprawl) is now a platform responsibility.
